text: The laparoscopic view shows the inflamed appendix with increased vascularity in a ten-year-old male patient, using three ports: a supra-umbilical camera port, and additional ports on the right and left sides.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to perform an appendectomy, applying a single western knot. The mesoappendix is separated at the base, with upward and medial traction applied to the appendix.

The surgeon observes the thickened appendix, measuring approximately eleven to twelve centimeters, in a ten-year-old boy. The inflamed appendix is visible, and the procedure is facilitated using a contralateral port.
The surgeon completes the separation of the appendix at its base and prepares to secure it with a single Western knot using a Y-curl suture.

The surgeon pulls the appendix toward the anterior abdominal wall and medially to place a western knot at its base. The visible stump is located below the knot, while the area above the knot, lacking vascularity, is expected to degenerate.

The surgeon uses a 4mm telescope to visualize the removal of the appendix through a 10mm port. The appendix is held longitudinally to prevent slipping, ensuring a secure grip during extraction. The consistency of the appendix is observed, appearing normal despite the surgical context.
The appendix is visibly extracted through the ten mm port, completing the appendectomy procedure.
